content.text: A fallback is an alternative behaviour that activates when the primary tool fails after retries are exhausted or the error is unrecoverable. Retries handle transient failures by repeating the same call. Fallbacks handle permanent or structural failures by switching to a different source, a cached result, a simplified operation, or a graceful explanation to the user.
— when tool A fails, call tool B instead (e.g., primary search API down → backup API).
— when no tool can satisfy the request, return a graceful response from the LLM's own knowledge or ask the user for help.
A fallback should degrade gracefully, not silently. The LLM should always know that a fallback occurred and why, so it can communicate the limitation accurately to the user.
The most direct fallback: if the primary tool raises an exception, try an alternative implementation of the same operation. Wrap both in the same interface so the dispatcher can call either.
Cached Fallback — Stale Data on Failure

For tools that retrieve data that changes slowly (product info, config settings, user profiles), a cache fallback serves the last known good value when the live API is unavailable. The LLM must be told the data may be stale.

When a tool is unavailable and no cache exists, the LLM can fall back to its own training knowledge for general questions. Signal this explicitly in the tool result so the LLM knows it should disclose the limitation.
Some tools have multiple fallback options in priority order: live API → cached result → simplified query → error. A cascading fallback chain tries each option in sequence.
method that chains runnables: if the primary raises, the next is tried. This works with LLMs, chains, and tools.

from langchain_openai import ChatOpenAI
from langchain_anthropic import ChatAnthropic
from langchain_core.messages import HumanMessage

# Primary: GPT-4o-mini; fallback: Claude Haiku (if OpenAI is down)
primary_llm = ChatOpenAI(model="gpt-4o-mini")
fallback_llm = ChatAnthropic(model="claude-haiku-4-5-20251001")

llm_with_fallback = primary_llm.with_fallbacks([fallback_llm])

response = llm_with_fallback.invoke([HumanMessage(content="Hello!")])
print(response.content)

# Tool-level fallback with a custom runnable
from langchain_core.runnables import RunnableLambda

primary_search = RunnableLambda(lambda q: tavily_search(q["query"]))
fallback_search = RunnableLambda(lambda q: duckduckgo_search(q["query"]))

search_with_fallback = primary_search.with_fallbacks(
    [fallback_search],
    exceptions_to_handle=(Exception,),
)

results = search_with_fallback.invoke({"query": "latest AI news"})
print(results)
Error + instruction to use own knowledge
Complex query fails; simpler variant may work
No fallback possible; user must be told
If a tool has been failing consistently, continuing to call it wastes time and delays the user. A
tracks recent failures and short-circuits to the fallback immediately — without attempting the primary — until the service recovers.
— normal operation. Calls go to the primary tool.
— primary is failing. Calls skip directly to the fallback.
— after a timeout, one test call probes whether the primary has recovered.

Fallbacks are valuable in production, but they must be observable. Silent fallbacks hide infrastructure problems: if your primary search API has been down for an hour and the fallback is serving all requests, you need to know.

Every fallback should annotate its result with a source label and a staleness or limitation note. The LLM needs this information to give the user an honest, accurate answer. A silent fallback that returns wrong data is worse than a clear error.
